返回本批次

八年的渴望，三个月的人工智能构建

Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI

lalitm.com 作者 brilee 分数 763 评论 229 2026/04/05 20:43
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 八年来，我一直想要一套高质量的开发工具来使用 SQLite。鉴于 SQLite 对行业的重要性1，我一直很困惑，没有人投资为其构建真正良好的开发人员体验2。几周前，经过三个月3 晚上、周末和假期约 250 个小时的努力，我终于发布了 syntaqlite (GitHub)，实现了这个长期的愿望。我认为发生这种情况的主要原因是人工智能编码代理4。当然，不乏声称人工智能一次性完成了他们的项目或反驳并宣称人工智能完全是马虎的帖子。我将采取一种非常不同的方法，相反，系统地分解我使用 AI 构建 syntaqlite 的经验，包括它的帮助和有害的地方。我将在将项目和我的背景置于背景中的同时这样做，以便您可以独立评估这种经验的普遍性。每当我提出主张时，我都会尝试用项目日志、编码记录或提交历史记录中的证据来支持它5。为什么我想要它在 Perfetto 的工作中，我维护一种基于 SQLite 的语言，用于查询性能跟踪，称为 PerfettoSQL。它与 SQLite 基本相同，但有一些扩展以使跟踪查询体验更好。 Google 内部大约有 10 万行 PerfettoSQL，并被广泛的团队使用。拥有一种受欢迎的语言意味着您的用户也开始期待诸如格式化程序、linter 和编辑器扩展之类的东西。我本来希望我们能够采用一些开源的 SQLite 工具，但我越研究它，我就越失望。我发现要么不够可靠、不够快6，要么不够灵活，无法适应 PerfettoSQL。显然有机会从头开始构建一些东西，但这从来都不是“我们可以做的最重要的事情”。我们一直不情愿地使用现有的工具，但总是希望有更好的。另一方面，我可以选择在业余时间做一些事情。我在十几岁的时候建立了很多开源项目，但在大学期间，当我觉得我不再有动力时，这些项目就消失了。作为维护者不仅仅是“把代码扔出去”然后看看会发生什么。它对错误进行分类，调查崩溃，编写文档，建立社区，最重要的是，为项目确定方向。但是开源的渴望（特别是在帮助他人的同时自由地做我想做的事情）从未消失。 SQLite devtools 项目在我心中永远是“我想要从事的事情”。但我一直推迟它还有另一个原因：它既困难又乏味。是什么让它变得困难和乏味如果我打算投入我的个人时间在这个项目上，我不想构建只帮助 Perfetto 的东西：我想让它为任何 SQLite 用户工作8。这意味着像 SQLite 一样解析 SQL。任何面向语言的开发工具的核心都是解析器。它负责将源代码转换为“解析树”，它充当构建其他任何内容的中央数据结构。如果你的解析器不准确，那么你的格式化程序和 linter 将不可避免地继承这些不准确之处；我发现的许多工具的解析器都近似于 SQLite 语言，而不是精确地表示它。不幸的是，与许多其他语言不同，SQLite 没有正式的规范来描述如何解析它。它也没有为其解析器公开稳定的 API 。事实上，非常独特的是，在其实现中，它甚至根本没有构建解析树9！在我看来，唯一合理的方法是仔细提取 SQLite 源代码的相关部分，并对其进行调整以构建我想要的解析器。这意味着深入了解 SQLite 源代码，这是一个极其难以理解的代码库。整个项目是用 C 语言编写的，风格非常密集；我花了几天时间来了解虚拟表 API11 和实现。试图掌握完整的解析器堆栈是令人畏惧的。事实上，SQLite 中有超过 400 条规则，可以捕获其语言的全部表面区域。我必须在每个“语法规则”中指定语法的该部分如何映射到解析树中的匹配节点。这是一项极其重复的工作；每条规则都与其周围的所有规则相似，但从定义上来说，也是不同的。这不仅仅是规则，还包括提出并编写测试以确保其正确，调试出现的问题，分类并修复人们在我出现问题时提出的不可避免的错误……多年来，这个想法就这样消失了。对于副业项目来说太难12，太乏味而无法维持动力，投入数月时间到可能行不通的事情上风险太大。它是如何发生的我自 2025 年初以来一直在使用编码代理（Aider，RooCode，然后是自 7 月份以来的 Claude Code），它们肯定很有用，但我觉得我从来没有觉得我可以信任一个严肃的项目。但到 2025 年底，这些模型似乎在质量方面取得了重大进步13。与此同时，我在 Perfetto 中不断遇到问题，如果有一个可靠的解析器，这些问题就可以轻松解决。每个解决方法都在我的脑海中留下了相同的想法：也许终于到了真正构建它的时候了。圣诞节期间我有一些空间来思考和反思，并决定真正对人工智能的最大版本进行压力测试：我可以仅使用 Max 计划（200 英镑/月）上的 Claude 代码对整个事情进行振动编码吗？在 1 月份的大部分时间里，我一直在迭代，充当半技术经理，并将几乎所有设计和所有实现都委托给 Claude。从功能上来说，我最终得到了一个合理的结果：一个使用一堆 Python 脚本从 SQLite 源代码中提取的 C 解析器、一个构建于其之上的格式化程序、对 SQLite 语言和 PerfettoSQL 扩展的支持，所有这些都在 Web Playground 中公开。但是，当我在 1 月底详细查看代码库时，缺点很明显：代码库完全是 spaghetti14。我不了解Python源代码提取管道的大部分内容，函数分散在随机文件中，没有清晰的形状，并且一些文件已经增长到数千行。它极其脆弱；它解决了眼前的问题，但它永远无法满足我更大的愿景，更不用说将其集成到 Perfetto 工具中了。可取之处在于，它证明了该方法是可行的，并生成了 500 多个测试，其中许多测试我觉得我可以重用。我决定扔掉所有东西并从头开始，同时还将大部分代码库切换到 Rust15。我可以看到 C 将使构建更高级别的组件（例如验证器和语言服务器实现）变得困难。作为奖励，它还允许我在提取和运行时使用相同的语言，而不是在 C 和 Python 之间拆分。更重要的是，我完全改变了我在项目中的角色。我掌握了所有决策16，并在更严格的流程中将其更多地用作“类固醇自动完成”：预先固执己见的设计，彻底审查每个更改，在发现问题时急切地解决问题，并投资于脚手架（例如 linting、验证和重要测试17）以自动检查 AI 输出。核心功能在 2 月份汇集在一起，最后的延伸（上游测试验证、编辑器扩展、打包、文档）导致 0.1 版本的发布三月中旬。但在我看来，这条时间线是这个故事中最无趣的部分。我真正想谈论的是，如果没有人工智能，一切就不会发生，以及它在我使用它时给我带来的损失。人工智能是这个项目存在的原因，也是它如此完整的原因。克服惰性我过去曾写过，作为一名软件工程师，我最大的弱点之一是在面对一个大的新项目时，我倾向于拖延。虽然我当时没有意识到，但它不能更完美地应用于构建 syntaqlite.AI 基本上让我抛开了对技术调用的所有疑虑，我对构建正确的东西的不确定性以及我不愿意通过给我提供非常具体的问题来解决。不是“我需要了解 SQLite 的解析是如何工作的”，而是“我需要让 AI 为我建议一种方法，这样我就可以拆掉它并构建更好的东西”18。与在脑海中无休止地思考设计相比，我使用具体的原型和代码来工作要好得多，而人工智能让我以我以前无法想象的速度达到这一点。一旦我迈出了第一步，之后的每一步都变得更加容易。 更快地搅动代码 事实证明，人工智能在编写代码本身方面比我更好，假设代码是显而易见的。如果我可以将问题分解为“编写一个具有此行为和参数的函数”或“编写一个与此接口匹配的类”，那么人工智能将比我更快地构建它，而且最重要的是，它的构建方式对未来的读者来说可能更直观。它记录了我会跳过的内容，与项目的其余部分保持一致地布置代码，并坚持您所使用的任何语言的“标准方言”19。这种标准是一把双刃剑。对于任何项目中的绝大多数代码，标准正是您想要的：可预测、可读、不足为奇。但每个项目都有其优势，即价值来自于做一些不明显的事情的部分。对于 syntaqlite 来说，这就是提取管道和解析器架构。人工智能标准化的本能在那里是非常有害的，而这些部分是我必须深入设计的部分，并且常常只能自己编写。但另一方面：人工智能在明显代码方面表现出色的速度也使其在重构方面表现出色。如果您使用人工智能要生成工业规模的代码，您必须不断地进行重构20。如果你不这样做，事情就会立即失控。这是振动编码月的中心教训：我重构得不够，代码库变得我无法推理，我不得不把它们全部扔掉。在重写中，重构成为了我工作流程的核心。每生成一大批代码后，我都会退后一步问“这丑吗？”有时人工智能可以清理它。其他时候，人工智能看不到大规模的抽象，但我可以；我会给它指示并让它执行21。如果你有品味，错误方法的成本会大幅下降，因为你可以快速重组22。助教在我使用人工智能的所有方式中，迄今为止，研究所带来的价值与所花费的时间的比率最高。我以前曾使用过解释器和解析器，但我从未听说过Wad​​ler-Lindig漂亮的打印23。当我需要构建格式化程序时，人工智能从我可以理解的角度给了我一个具体且可操作的课程，并为我指明了论文以了解更多信息。我本可以自己最终找到这一点，但人工智能将可能需要一两天的阅读内容压缩为一次集中对话，我可以在对话中问“但这为什么有效？”直到我真正得到它。这扩展到了我从未工作过的整个领域。我拥有深厚的 C++ 和 Android 性能专业知识，但几乎没有接触过 Rust 工具或编辑器扩展 API。对于人工智能来说，这不是问题：基本原理相同，术语相似，人工智能弥补了差距24。在开始之前，VS Code 扩展需要我花一两天的时间来学习 API。借助人工智能，我在一小时内获得了工作延期。这对于重新熟悉我几天没有查看的项目部分内容也非常宝贵25。我可以控制深入程度：“告诉我这个组件”以进行表面复习，“给我一个详细的线性演练”以进行更深入的研究，“审核此存储库中的不安全用法”以寻找问题。当你频繁切换上下文时，你会很快失去上下文。人工智能让我可以根据需要重新获取它。比我单独构建的还要多 除了使该项目存在之外，人工智能也是它交付得如此完整的原因。每个开源项目都有一个长尾功能，这些功能很重要但不是关键：你理论上知道如何做但不断降低优先级的事情，因为核心工作更紧迫。对于 syntaqlite，这个列表很长：编辑器扩展、Python 绑定、WASM 游乐场、文档网站、多个生态系统的打包26。 AI 让这些变得足够便宜，以至于跳过它们感觉像是一个错误的权衡。它还为 UX27 释放了精神能量。我不用把所有时间都花在实现上，而是可以考虑用户的第一次体验应该是什么样的：哪些错误消息实际上可以帮助他们修复 SQL，默认情况下格式化程序输出应该是什么样子，CLI 标志是否直观。这些是将人们尝试过一次的工具与他们一直使用的工具区分开来的东西，而人工智能给了我关心它们的空间。如果没有人工智能，我会构建更小的东西，可能没有编辑器扩展或文档网站。人工智能不仅仅让同一个项目变得更快。它改变了项目的内容。人工智能的成本在哪里上瘾使用人工智能编码工具和玩老虎机之间存在着令人不安的相似之处28。你发送一个提示，等待，要么得到一些很棒的东西，要么得到一些无用的东西。我发现自己在深夜醒来，想要“再做一次提示”，不断尝试人工智能，只是为了看看会发生什么，即使我知道它可能行不通。沉没成本谬误也开始出现：即使在明显不适合的任务中，我也会坚持下去，告诉自己“也许如果我这次换一种说法。”疲劳反馈循环让情况变得更糟29。当我有精力时，我可以写出精确、范围明确的提示，并且真正高效。但当我累了的时候，我的提示变得模糊，输出变得更糟，我会再试一次，在这个过程中变得更累。在这些情况下，人工智能可能比我自己实现一些东西要慢，但打破循环太难了30。在项目过程中几次失去联系，我失去了代码库的心理模型31。不是整体架构或事物如何组合在一起。但日常细节包括什么在哪里、什么功能称为什么，以及累积到工作系统中的小决定。当这种情况发生时，令人惊讶的问题就会出现，我会发现自己完全不知道出了什么问题。我讨厌这种感觉。更深层次的问题是失去联系会导致沟通中断32。当您不了解正在发生的事情时，就不可能与代理进行有意义的沟通。每次交换都会变得更长、更冗长。你最终会说“改变这个东西”，而不是“改变 FooClass 来做 X”然后代理必须弄清楚 Bar 是什么，如何映射到 FooClass，有时它会出错33。这与工程师一直对不理解代码的经理提出的抱怨完全一样，要求做一些奇特或不可能的事情。只不过现在你已经成为了那个经理。修复是经过深思熟虑的：我养成了一种习惯，在代码实现后立即通读代码，并积极参与看看“我会如何以不同的方式完成这件事？”。当然，在某些情况下，感觉上述所有内容也适用于我几个月前编写的代码（因此人们认为人工智能代码是遗留代码），但人工智能使漂移发生得更快，因为你没有建立与最初输入代码相同的肌肉记忆。缓慢的腐蚀还有一些其他问题我只是在三个月内逐渐发现。我发现人工智能让我在关键设计决策上拖延34，因为重构成本很低，我总是可以说“我稍后会处理这个问题。”而且由于人工智能可以以与生成代码相同的工业规模进行重构，因此推迟的成本感觉很低。但事实并非如此：推迟决策削弱了我清晰思考的能力，因为与此同时代码库仍然令人困惑。振动编码月是最极端的版本。是的，我理解这个问题，但如果我更早地进行硬设计调用，我可以更快地收敛到正确的架构。测试产生了类似的虚假安慰35。进行 500 多次测试让人感到安心，而人工智能可以轻松生成更多测试。但人类和人工智能都没有足够的创造力来预见未来会遇到的每种极端情况；在振动编码阶段，我有好几次提出一个测试用例，并意识到某些组件的设计是完全错误的，需要完全重新设计。这是我缺乏信任并决定放弃一切并从头开始的一个重要原因。基本上，我了解到软件的“正常规则”仍然适用于人工智能时代：如果你没有基本的基础（清晰的架构，明确的边界），你将永远追逐它们出现的错误。没有时间感我一直回想起的是人工智能对时间的流逝了解得多么少36。它看到处于某种状态的代码库，但感觉时间的方式与人类不同。我可以告诉你使用 API 的感觉，它是如何在数月或数年内演变的，为什么做出某些决定然后又被推翻。缺乏理解的自然问题是，你要么犯过去犯过的同样的错误，必须重新吸取教训，要么你陷入第一次成功避免的新陷阱，从长远来看，会减慢你的速度。在我看来，这与失去一名高素质的高级工程师对团队造成如此大的伤害是一个类似的问题：他们拥有其他任何地方都不存在的历史和背景，并为周围的其他人提供指导。理论上，您可以尝试通过更新规范和文档来保留这种背景。但在人工智能出现之前，我们没有这样做是有原因的：彻底捕获隐式设计决策的成本非常高，而且记录下来也非常耗时。人工智能可以帮助起草这些文档，但由于无法自动验证它是否准确捕获了重要内容，因此人类仍然必须手动审核结果。这仍然很耗时。还有上下文污染问题。你永远不知道关于 API A 的设计说明何时会在 API B 中得到回应。一致性是代码库工作的重要组成部分，为此，你不仅需要有关你现在正在处理的内容的上下文，还需要有关以类似方式设计的其他内容的上下文。决定什么是相关的，首先需要机构知识提供的那种判断。相对论反思上述情况，人工智能何时提供帮助、何时造成伤害的模式相当一致。当我从事我已经深入理解的事情时，人工智能非常出色。我可以立即查看其输出，在错误发生之前发现它们，并以我独自无法完成的速度前进。解析器规则生成是最清晰的例子37：我确切地知道每个规则应该生成什么，因此我可以在一两分钟内查看人工智能的输出并快速迭代。当我正在研究一些我可以描述但还不知道的东西时，人工智能很好，但需要更多的照顾。学习格式化程序的 Wadler-Lindig 是这样的：我可以清楚地表达我想要什么，评估输出是否朝着正确的方向前进，并从人工智能的解释中学习。但我必须保持投入，不能只是接受它给我的东西。当我在做一些我什至不知道自己想要什么的事情时，人工智能介于无益和有害之间。该项目的架构是最清晰的案例：早期我花了数周时间跟踪人工智能的死胡同，探索感觉富有成效的设计在那一刻却在审视下崩溃了。事后看来，我不得不想，在没有人工智能参与的情况下，仅仅思考是否会更快。但仅靠专业知识是不够的。即使我深入理解了一个问题，如果任务没有客观可检查的答案，人工智能仍然会陷入困境38。实现有一个正确的答案，至少在本地级别：代码编译，测试通过，输出符合您的要求。设计没有。在 OOP 首次起飞几十年后，我们仍在争论 OOP。 具体来说，我发现设计 syntaqlite 的公共 API 是最难解决的问题。三月初，我花了几天时间，除了 API 重构、手动修复任何有经验的工程师本能地避免但人工智能把事情弄得一团糟的事情。对于“这个 API 用起来是否愉快”和“这个 API 能否帮助用户解决他们遇到的问题”没有测试或客观指标，这正是编码代理在这方面做得如此糟糕的原因。这让我回到了我痴迷于物理学，特别是相对论的日子。在任何小的局部区域中，物理定律看起来都很简单且符合牛顿定律，但缩小时空曲线的方式是您无法仅从局部图像中预测的。代码是一样的：在函数或类的层面上，通常有一个明确的正确答案，而人工智能在这方面非常出色。但架构是当所有这些本地部分相互作用时发生的事情，并且你无法通过将本地正确的组件拼接在一起来获得良好的全局行为。我认为，在任何给定时刻知道你在这些轴上的位置是有效使用人工智能的核心技能。总结八年对于你头脑中的一个项目来说是一段很长的时间。在仅仅三个月的工作后看到这些 SQLite 工具实际存在并发挥作用是一个巨大的胜利，我完全意识到如果没有人工智能，它们就不会出现。但这个过程并不是人们通常发布的干净、线性的成功故事。我花了整整一个月的时间来进行振动编码。我陷入了管理一个我实际上并不理解的代码库的陷阱，并为此付出了彻底重写的代价。对我来说，要点很简单：人工智能是实现方面令人难以置信的力量倍增器，但它是设计的危险替代品。它很擅长为您提供特定技术问题的正确答案，但它没有历史感、品味，也没有人类使用 API 时的实际感受。如果你依赖它作为你的软件的“灵魂”，你最终会比以前更快地碰壁。我希望从其他人那里看到更多的正是我在这里尝试做的：使用这些工具构建真实软件的诚实、详细的描述；不是周末玩具或一次性脚本，而是一种必须经得起与用户接触、错误报告和您自己不断变化的想法的软件。

Original Summary

  1. For eight years, I’ve wanted a high-quality set of devtools for working with SQLite. Given how important SQLite is to the industry1, I’ve long been puzzled that no one has invested in building a really good developer experience for it2.A couple of weeks ago, after ~250 hours of effort over three months3 on evenings, weekends, and vacation days, I finally released syntaqlite (GitHub), fulfilling this long-held wish. And I believe the main reason this happened was because of AI coding agents4.Of course, there’s no shortage of posts claiming that AI one-shot their project or pushing back and declaring that AI is all slop. I’m going to take a very different approach and, instead, systematically break down my experience building syntaqlite with AI, both where it helped and where it was detrimental.I’ll do this while contextualizing the project and my background so you can independently assess how generalizable this experience was. And whenever I make a claim, I’ll try to back it up with evidence from my project journal, coding transcripts, or commit history5.Why I wanted itIn my work on Perfetto, I maintain a SQLite-based language for querying performance traces called PerfettoSQL. It’s basically the same as SQLite but with a few extensions to make the trace querying experience better. There are ~100K lines of PerfettoSQL internally in Google and it’s used by a wide range of teams.Having a language which gets traction means your users also start expecting things like formatters, linters, and editor extensions. I’d hoped that we could adapt some SQLite tools from open source but the more I looked into it, the more disappointed I was. What I found either wasn’t reliable enough, fast enough6, or flexible enough to adapt to PerfettoSQL. There was clearly an opportunity to build something from scratch, but it was never the “most important thing we could work on”. We’ve been reluctantly making do with the tools out there but always wishing for better.On the other hand, there was the option to do something in my spare time. I had built lots of open source projects in my teens7 but this had faded away during university when I felt that I just didn’t have the motivation anymore. Being a maintainer is much more than just “throwing the code out there” and seeing what happens. It’s triaging bugs, investigating crashes, writing documentation, building a community, and, most importantly, having a direction for the project.But the itch of open source (specifically freedom to work on what I wanted while helping others) had never gone away. The SQLite devtools project was eternally in my mind as “something I’d like to work on”. But there was another reason why I kept putting it off: it sits at the intersection of being both hard and tedious.What makes it hard and tediousIf I was going to invest my personal time working on this project, I didn’t want to build something that only helped Perfetto: I wanted to make it work for any SQLite user out there8. And this means parsing SQL exactly like SQLite.The heart of any language-oriented devtool is the parser. This is responsible for turning the source code into a “parse tree” which acts as the central data structure anything else is built on top of. If your parser isn’t accurate, then your formatters and linters will inevitably inherit those inaccuracies; many of the tools I found suffered from having parsers which approximated the SQLite language rather than representing it precisely.Unfortunately, unlike many other languages, SQLite has no formal specification describing how it should be parsed. It doesn’t expose a stable API for its parser either. In fact, quite uniquely, in its implementation it doesn’t even build a parse tree at all9! The only reasonable approach left in my opinion is to carefully extract the relevant parts of SQLite’s source code and adapt it to build the parser I wanted.This means getting into the weeds of SQLite source code, a fiendishly difficult codebase to understand. The whole project is written in C in an incredibly dense style; I’ve spent days just understanding the virtual table API11 and implementation. Trying to grasp the full parser stack was daunting.There’s also the fact that there are >400 rules in SQLite which capture the full surface area of its language. I’d have to specify in each of these “grammar rules” how that part of the syntax maps to the matching node in the parse tree. It’s extremely repetitive work; each rule is similar to all the ones around it but also, by definition, different.And it’s not just the rules but also coming up with and writing tests to make sure it’s correct, debugging if something is wrong, triaging and fixing the inevitable bugs people filed when I got something wrong…For years, this was where the idea died. Too hard for a side project12, too tedious to sustain motivation, too risky to invest months into something that might not work.How it happenedI’ve been using coding agents since early 2025 (Aider, Roo Code, then Claude Code since July) and they’d definitely been useful but never something I felt I could trust a serious project to. But towards the end of 2025, the models seemed to make a significant step forward in quality13. At the same time, I kept hitting problems in Perfetto which would have been trivially solved by having a reliable parser. Each workaround left the same thought in the back of my mind: maybe it’s finally time to build it for real.I got some space to think and reflect over Christmas and decided to really stress test the most maximalist version of AI: could I vibe-code the whole thing using just Claude Code on the Max plan (£200/month)?Through most of January, I iterated, acting as semi-technical manager and delegating almost all the design and all the implementation to Claude. Functionally, I ended up in a reasonable place: a parser in C extracted from SQLite sources using a bunch of Python scripts, a formatter built on top, support for both the SQLite language and the PerfettoSQL extensions, all exposed in a web playground.But when I reviewed the codebase in detail in late January, the downside was obvious: the codebase was complete spaghetti14. I didn’t understand large parts of the Python source extraction pipeline, functions were scattered in random files without a clear shape, and a few files had grown to several thousand lines. It was extremely fragile; it solved the immediate problem but it was never going to cope with my larger vision, never mind integrating it into the Perfetto tools. The saving grace was that it had proved the approach was viable and generated more than 500 tests, many of which I felt I could reuse.I decided to throw away everything and start from scratch while also switching most of the codebase to Rust15. I could see that C was going to make it difficult to build the higher level components like the validator and the language server implementation. And as a bonus, it would also let me use the same language for both the extraction and runtime instead of splitting it across C and Python.More importantly, I completely changed my role in the project. I took ownership of all decisions16 and used it more as “autocomplete on steroids” inside a much tighter process: opinionated design upfront, reviewing every change thoroughly, fixing problems eagerly as I spotted them, and investing in scaffolding (like linting, validation, and non-trivial testing17) to check AI output automatically.The core features came together through February and the final stretch (upstream test validation, editor extensions, packaging, docs) led to a 0.1 launch in mid-March.But in my opinion, this timeline is the least interesting part of this story. What I really want to talk about is what wouldn’t have happened without AI and also the toll it took on me as I used it.AI is why this project exists, and why it’s as complete as it isOvercoming inertiaI’ve written in the past about how one of my biggest weaknesses as a software engineer is my tendency to procrastinate when facing a big new project. Though I didn’t realize it at the time, it could not have applied more perfectly to building syntaqlite.AI basically let me put aside all my doubts on technical calls, my uncertainty of building the right thing and my reluctance to get started by giving me very concrete problems to work on. Instead of “I need to understand how SQLite’s parsing works”, it was “I need to get AI to suggest an approach for me so I can tear it up and build something better"18. I work so much better with concrete prototypes to play with and code to look at than endlessly thinking about designs in my head, and AI lets me get to that point at a pace I could not have dreamed about before. Once I took the first step, every step after that was so much easier.Faster at churning codeAI turned out to be better than me at the act of writing code itself, assuming that code is obvious. If I can break a problem down to “write a function with this behaviour and parameters” or “write a class matching this interface,” AI will build it faster than I would and, crucially, in a style that might well be more intuitive to a future reader. It documents things I’d skip, lays out code consistently with the rest of the project, and sticks to what you might call the “standard dialect” of whatever language you’re working in19.That standardness is a double-edged sword. For the vast majority of code in any project, standard is exactly what you want: predictable, readable, unsurprising. But every project has pieces that are its edge, the parts where the value comes from doing something non-obvious. For syntaqlite, that was the extraction pipeline and the parser architecture. AI’s instinct to normalize was actively harmful there, and those were the parts I had to design in depth and often resorted to just writing myself.But here’s the flip side: the same speed that makes AI great at obvious code also makes it great at refactoring. If you’re using AI to generate code at industrial scale, you have to refactor constantly and continuously20. If you don’t, things immediately get out of hand. This was the central lesson of the vibe-coding month: I didn’t refactor enough, the codebase became something I couldn’t reason about, and I had to throw it all away. In the rewrite, refactoring became the core of my workflow. After every large batch of generated code, I’d step back and ask “is this ugly?” Sometimes AI could clean it up. Other times there was a large-scale abstraction that AI couldn’t see but I could; I’d give it the direction and let it execute21. If you have taste, the cost of a wrong approach drops dramatically because you can restructure quickly22.Teaching assistantOf all the ways I used AI, research had by far the highest ratio of value delivered to time spent.I’ve worked with interpreters and parsers before but I had never heard of Wadler-Lindig pretty printing23. When I needed to build the formatter, AI gave me a concrete and actionable lesson from a point of view I could understand and pointed me to the papers to learn more. I could have found this myself eventually, but AI compressed what might have been a day or two of reading into a focused conversation where I could ask “but why does this work?” until I actually got it.This extended to entire domains I’d never worked in. I have deep C++ and Android performance expertise but had barely touched Rust tooling or editor extension APIs. With AI, it wasn’t a problem: the fundamentals are the same, the terminology is similar, and AI bridges the gap24. The VS Code extension would have taken me a day or two of learning the API before I could even start. With AI, I had a working extension within an hour.It was also invaluable for reacquainting myself with parts of the project I hadn’t looked at for a few days25. I could control how deep to go: “tell me about this component” for a surface-level refresher, “give me a detailed linear walkthrough” for a deeper dive, “audit unsafe usages in this repo” to go hunting for problems. When you’re context switching a lot, you lose context fast. AI let me reacquire it on demand.More than I’d have built aloneBeyond making the project exist at all, AI is also the reason it shipped as complete as it did. Every open source project has a long tail of features that are important but not critical: the things you know theoretically how to do but keep deprioritizing because the core work is more pressing. For syntaqlite, that list was long: editor extensions, Python bindings, a WASM playground, a docs site, packaging for multiple ecosystems26. AI made these cheap enough that skipping them felt like the wrong trade-off.It also freed up mental energy for UX27. Instead of spending all my time on implementation, I could think about what a user’s first experience should feel like: what error messages would actually help them fix their SQL, how the formatter output should look by default, whether the CLI flags were intuitive. These are the things that separate a tool people try once from one they keep using, and AI gave me the headroom to care about them. Without AI, I would have built something much smaller, probably no editor extensions or docs site. AI didn’t just make the same project faster. It changed what the project was.Where AI had its costsThe addictionThere’s an uncomfortable parallel between using AI coding tools and playing slot machines28. You send a prompt, wait, and either get something great or something useless. I found myself up late at night wanting to do “just one more prompt,” constantly trying AI just to see what would happen even when I knew it probably wouldn’t work. The sunk cost fallacy kicked in too: I’d keep at it even in tasks it was clearly ill-suited for, telling myself “maybe if I phrase it differently this time.”The tiredness feedback loop made it worse29. When I had energy, I could write precise, well-scoped prompts and be genuinely productive. But when I was tired, my prompts became vague, the output got worse, and I’d try again, getting more tired in the process. In these cases, AI was probably slower than just implementing something myself, but it was too hard to break out of the loop30.Losing touchSeveral times during the project, I lost my mental model of the codebase31. Not the overall architecture or how things fitted together. But the day-to-day details of what lived where, which functions called which, the small decisions that accumulate into a working system. When that happened, surprising issues would appear and I’d find myself at a total loss to understand what was going wrong. I hated that feeling.The deeper problem was that losing touch created a communication breakdown32. When you don’t have the mental thread of what’s going on, it becomes impossible to communicate meaningfully with the agent. Every exchange gets longer and more verbose. Instead of “change FooClass to do X,” you end up saying “change the thing which does Bar to do X”. Then the agent has to figure out what Bar is, how that maps to FooClass, and sometimes it gets it wrong33. It’s exactly the same complaint engineers have always had about managers who don’t understand the code asking for fanciful or impossible things. Except now you’ve become that manager.The fix was deliberate: I made it a habit to read through the code immediately after it was implemented and actively engage to see “how would I have done this differently?”.Of course, in some sense all of the above is also true of code I wrote a few months ago (hence the sentiment that AI code is legacy code), but AI makes the drift happen faster because you’re not building the same muscle memory that comes from originally typing it out.The slow corrosionThere were some other problems I only discovered incrementally over the three months.I found that AI made me procrastinate on key design decisions34. Because refactoring was cheap, I could always say “I’ll deal with this later.” And because AI could refactor at the same industrial scale it generated code, the cost of deferring felt low. But it wasn’t: deferring decisions corroded my ability to think clearly because the codebase stayed confusing in the meantime. The vibe-coding month was the most extreme version of this. Yes, I understood the problem, but if I had been more disciplined about making hard design calls earlier, I could have converged on the right architecture much faster.Tests created a similar false comfort35. Having 500+ tests felt reassuring, and AI made it easy to generate more. But neither humans nor AI are creative enough to foresee every edge case you’ll hit in the future; there are several times in the vibe-coding phase where I’d come up with a test case and realise the design of some component was completely wrong and needed to be totally reworked. This was a significant contributor to my lack of trust and the decision to scrap everything and start from scratch.Basically, I learned that the “normal rules” of software still apply in the AI age: if you don’t have a fundamental foundation (clear architecture, well-defined boundaries) you’ll be left eternally chasing bugs as they appear.No sense of timeSomething I kept coming back to was how little AI understood about the passage of time36. It sees a codebase in a certain state but doesn’t feel time the way humans do. I can tell you what it feels like to use an API, how it evolved over months or years, why certain decisions were made and later reversed.The natural problem from this lack of understanding is that you either make the same mistakes you made in the past and have to relearn the lessons or you fall into new traps which were successfully avoided the first time, slowing you down in the long run. In my opinion, this is a similar problem to why losing a high-quality senior engineer hurts a team so much: they carry history and context that doesn’t exist anywhere else and act as a guide for others around them.In theory, you can try to preserve this context by keeping specs and docs up to date. But there’s a reason we didn’t do this before AI: capturing implicit design decisions exhaustively is incredibly expensive and time-consuming to write down. AI can help draft these docs, but because there’s no way to automatically verify that it accurately captured what matters, a human still has to manually audit the result. And that’s still time-consuming.There’s also the context pollution problem. You never know when a design note about API A will echo in API B. Consistency is a huge part of what makes codebases work, and for that you don’t just need context about what you’re working on right now but also about other things which were designed in a similar way. Deciding what’s relevant requires exactly the kind of judgement that institutional knowledge provides in the first place.RelativityReflecting on the above, the pattern of when AI helped and when it hurt was fairly consistent.When I was working on something I already understood deeply, AI was excellent. I could review its output instantly, catch mistakes before they landed and move at a pace I’d never have managed alone. The parser rule generation is the clearest example37: I knew exactly what each rule should produce, so I could review AI’s output within a minute or two and iterate fast.When I was working on something I could describe but didn’t yet know, AI was good but required more care. Learning Wadler-Lindig for the formatter was like this: I could articulate what I wanted, evaluate whether the output was heading in the right direction, and learn from what AI explained. But I had to stay engaged and couldn’t just accept what it gave me.When I was working on something where I didn’t even know what I wanted, AI was somewhere between unhelpful and harmful. The architecture of the project was the clearest case: I spent weeks in the early days following AI down dead ends, exploring designs that felt productive in the moment but collapsed under scrutiny. In hindsight, I have to wonder if it would have been faster just thinking it through without AI in the loop at all.But expertise alone isn’t enough. Even when I understood a problem deeply, AI still struggled if the task had no objectively checkable answer38. Implementation has a right answer, at least at a local level: the code compiles, the tests pass, the output matches what you asked for. Design doesn’t. We’re still arguing about OOP decades after it first took off.Concretely, I found that designing the public API of syntaqlite was where this hit home the hardest. I spent several days in early March doing nothing but API refactoring, manually fixing things any experienced engineer would have instinctively avoided but AI made a total mess of. There’s no test or objective metric for “is this API pleasant to use” and “will this API help users solve the problems they have” and that’s exactly why the coding agents did so badly at it.This takes me back to the days I was obsessed with physics and, specifically, relativity. The laws of physics look simple and Newtonian in any small local area, but zoom out and spacetime curves in ways you can’t predict from the local picture alone. Code is the same: at the level of a function or a class, there’s usually a clear right answer, and AI is excellent there. But architecture is what happens when all those local pieces interact, and you can’t get good global behaviour by stitching together locally correct components.Knowing where you are on these axes at any given moment is, I think, the core skill of working with AI effectively.Wrap-upEight years is a long time to carry a project in your head. Seeing these SQLite tools actually exist and function after only three months of work is a massive win, and I’m fully aware they wouldn’t be here without AI.But the process wasn’t the clean, linear success story people usually post. I lost an entire month to vibe-coding. I fell into the trap of managing a codebase I didn’t actually understand, and I paid for that with a total rewrite.The takeaway for me is simple: AI is an incredible force multiplier for implementation, but it’s a dangerous substitute for design. It’s brilliant at giving you the right answer to a specific technical question, but it has no sense of history, taste, or how a human will actually feel using your API. If you rely on it for the “soul” of your software, you’ll just end up hitting a wall faster than you ever have before.What I’d like to see more of from others is exactly what I’ve tried to do here: honest, detailed accounts of building real software with these tools; not weekend toys or one-off scripts but the kind of software that has to survive contact with users, bug reports, and your own changing mind.

评论（默认中文）

Aurornis 2026/04/05 22:55
对人工智能编码的诚实和平衡的看法令人耳目一新。一旦你克服了让人工智能编写执行并执行你所要求的代码的最初令人惊叹的因素，这就是真正的人工智能辅助编码的样子。

对于每一位使用过 AI 代码生成并审查输出的认真的软件工程师来说，这种经历都很熟悉：

> 但当我在一月下旬详细审查代码库时，缺点很明显：代码库完全是意大利面条14。我不了解Python源代码提取管道的大部分内容，函数分散在随机文件中，没有清晰的形状，并且一些文件已经增长到数千行。它极其脆弱；它解决了眼前的问题，但它永远无法应对我更大的愿景，

有些人永远不会进入审查代码的部分。他们直接访问 LinkedIn 或博客，开始撰写（或让 ChatGPT 撰写）帖子，讲述手动编码如何消亡，并且他们将永远不再手工编写代码。

有些人审查代码并宣布它是不可用的垃圾，然后还在他们的社交媒体上发布人工智能编码完全无用的信息，他们不会将其用于任何用途。

这篇博文展示了不属于这两个少数派之一的任何人现在正在经历的旅程：认识到人工智能编码工具可以成为一个大型加速器，但您需要学习如何在工作流程中正确使用它们，并且需要继续参与代码。它并不像一直发布的极端言论那样吸引点击。读到他们说仍然需要努力工作的部分有点令人失望。不过，这是对人工智能编码现状的现实且平衡的看法。

Refreshing to see an honest and balanced take on AI coding. This is what real AI-assisted coding looks like once you get past the initial wow factor of having the AI write code that executes and does what you asked.

This experience is familiar to every serious software engineer who has used AI code gen and then reviewed the output:

> But when I reviewed the codebase in detail in late January, the downside was obvious: the codebase was complete spaghetti14. I didn’t understand large parts of the Python source extraction pipeline, functions were scattered in random files without a clear shape, and a few files had grown to several thousand lines. It was extremely fragile; it solved the immediate problem but it was never going to cope with my larger vision,

Some people never get to the part where they review the code. They go straight to their LinkedIn or blog and start writing (or having ChatGPT write) posts about how manual coding is dead and they’re done writing code by hand forever.

Some people review the code and declare it unusable garbage, then also go to their social media and post how AI coding is completely useless and they’re not going to use it for anything.

This blog post shows the journey that anyone not in one of those two vocal minorities is going through right now: A realization that AI coding tools can be a large accelerator but you need to learn how to use them correctly in your workflow and you need to remain involved in the code. It’s not as clickbaity as the extreme takes that get posted all the time. It’s a little disappointing to read the part where they said hard work was still required. It is a realistic and balanced take on the state of AI coding, though.

在 HN 中查看
yojo 2026/04/06 00:23
+1

在过去的三个月里，我一直在使用 Claude 作为我的主要编码界面。除了不同的领域之外，我觉得我本来可以写这篇文章。

我正在进行的项目最初是一个振动编码原型，很快就升级为我们销售的生产服务。

我必须在事后建立心理模型，同时重构和删除大量无意义或死代码。

但如果没有那个快速而肮脏的原型，这个产品就不会存在，我可以用克劳德作为该死的电锯来清理。

周五，我终于添加了一个类型检查器预提交挂钩，并在大约 2 小时内修复了 90 个现有错误（正确地说，没有类型被忽略）。我首先尝试了完全代理，但它惨遭失败，然后我和 Claude 一起经历了一个又一个错误，我们收紧了一些现有类型，修复了一些笨重的抽象，并得到了一个漂亮、干净的结果。

人工智能辅助编码非常棒，但在我看来，对于生产代码来说，人工审查和指导是无法替代的。

+1

I’ve been driving Claude as my primary coding interface the last three months at my job. Other than a different domain, I feel like I could have written this exact article.

The project I’m on started as a vibe-coded prototype that quickly got promoted to a production service we sell.

I’ve had to build the mental model after the fact, while refactoring and ripping out large chunks of nonsense or dead code.

But the product wouldn’t exist without that quick and dirty prototype, and I can use Claude as a goddamned chainsaw to clean up.

On Friday, I finally added a type checker pre-commit hook and fixed the 90 existing errors (properly, no type ignores) in ~2 hours. I tried full-agentic first, and it failed miserably, then I went through error by error with Claude, we tightened up some exiting types, fixed some clunky abstractions, and got a nice, clean result.

AI-assisted coding is amazing, but IMO for production code there’s no substitute for human review and guidance.

在 HN 中查看
figassis 2026/04/06 02:02
我的流程是：开始构思，让人工智能在你的推理、愿景、可扩展性等方面找出漏洞。在休息的时候这样做几天。这一切都包含在一个包含美人鱼图和部分的 Md 文件中。

然后使用构思来架构，深入细节并准确地告诉 AI 你的选择是什么，应该如何调用某些方法，应该如何设置日志记录和可观察性，使用什么语言，类型检查，编码风格（在编写一行代码之前配置无情的 linting 和格式化），什么测试方法，框架，单元，集成，e2e。数据库，尽可能多地更改您将处理的迁移，以便人工智能尽可能限制您的操作方式。

然后，创建一个计划文件，让它像任务列表一样管理它，并分批实施，在开始之前需要给你一个计划，在里面你会发现它会犯错误，误解一些你之前可能没有澄清的事情，或者它会忘记。您添加到 AGENTS.md 或其他内容，对 ai 的计划进行更改，告诉它更新 plan.md，当满意时，继续。

完成后，检查代码。你会发现总有一些东西需要修复。硬编码变量，带有种子数据的 SQL 迁移实际上不应该是迁移，只是通常疯狂的东西。

最糟糕的是，AI 的要求总是非常宽松。您会注意到它的所有字段都是可为空的，记录几乎没有验证，您在测试时报告错误，并且它尝试使用脆弱的异步解决方案（例如 LISTEN/NOTIFY 或回调）来解决它，而不是执行架构上正确的解决方案。大规模的事情很难调试，尤其是如果你没有编写代码的话。

如果你这样做并迭代，你将逐渐获得一个可靠的工具，并且你将需要更少的审查。

然后将其移植到其他项目中。

My process: start ideating and get the AI to poke holes in your reasoning, your vision, scalability, etc. do this for a few days while taking breaks. This is all contained in one Md file with mermaid diagrams and sections.

Then use ideation to architect, dive into details and tell the AI exactly what your choices are, how certain methods should be called, how logging and observability should be setup, what language to use, type checking, coding style (configure ruthless linting and formatting before you write a single line of code), what testing methodology, framework, unit, integration, e2e. Database, changes you will handle migrations, as much as possible so the AI is as confined as possible to how you would do it.

Then, create a plan file, have it manage it like a task list, and implement in parts, before starting it needs to present you a plan, in it you will notice it will make mistakes, misunderstand some things that you may me didn’t clarify before, or it will just forget. You add to AGENTS.md or whatever, make changes to the ai’s plan, tell it to update the plan.md and when satisfied, proceed.

After done, review the code. You will notice there is always something to fix. Hardcoded variables, a sql migration with seed data that should actually not be a migration, just generally crazy stuff.

The worst is that the AI is always very loose on requirements. You will notice all its fields are nullable, records have little to no validation, you report an error when testing and it tried to solve it with an brittle async solution, like LISTEN/NOTIFY or a callback instead of doing the architecturally correct solution. Things that at scale are hell to debug, especially if you did not write the code.

If you do this and iterate you will gradually end up with a solid harness and you will need to review less.

Then port it to other projects.

LISTEN/NOTIFY 并不脆弱，我们每天将它用于数百万个事件。
LISTEN/NOTIFY is not brittle, we use it for millions of events per day.

JoelJacobson 2026/04/06 14:55
我同意！ IMO，它应该非常稳定。如果没有，请发送错误报告，我们将进行调查。此外，现在它可以随着侦听连接的数量很好地扩展（假设客户端侦听唯一的通道名称）： https://git.postgresql.org/gitweb/?p=postgresql.git;a=commit...
I agree! It should be very stable, IMO. If not, then please send a bug report and we'll look into it. Also, now it scales well with the number of listening connections (given clients listen on unique channel names): https://git.postgresql.org/gitweb/?p=postgresql.git;a=commit...

我发现很有趣的是，您假设这种方法会扩展到其他项目。我发现更有趣的是，您假设所有软件代码库都使用数据库，对异步任何东西都非常在意，并且这些想法渗透到通用软件工程中。

听起来像是制作粗略的网络应用程序的可靠方法。

I find it very interesting that you assume this method would branch out to other projects. I find it even more interesting that you assume all software codebases use a database, give a damn about async anything, and that these ideas percolate out to general software engineering.

在 HN 中查看
klardotsh 2026/04/06 09:59
GP 清楚地提供了任务类别的示例。当然，并非所有语言都执行“async fn foo()”，但几乎所有问题域都涉及某种确保在正确的时间发生正确的事情，这是一个类似的过程。

比你更神圣“是的，好吧 我 从事不使用数据库的工作，将死！”并没有真正落地——数据仍然会以某种方式移动，而且通常是通过网络！

GP is clearly providing examples of categories of tasks. Sure, not all languages do “async fn foo()”, but almost all problem domains involve some sort of making sure the right things happen at the right times, which is in a similar ballpark.

Holier than thou “yeah well I work on stuff that doesn’t use databases, checkmate!” doesn’t really land - data still gets moved around somehow, and often over a network!

59nadir 2026/04/06 14:57
> 他可能会再次越来越倾向于振动编码

我认为“越来越多”在这里做了一些非常繁重的工作。我认为，从表面上看，对很多人来说，它读起来像是“很多”，这就是为什么读起来很难不感到有点畏缩。读起来就像“这非常容易上瘾，最终你会再次接受胡言乱语，但我还没有意识到这就是发生的事情”。

但事实是，这个评论实际上完全依赖于这里“越来越多”的含义。

> he'll likely go more and more towards vibecoding again

I think "more and more" is doing some very heavy lifting here. On the surface it reads like "a lot" to many people, I think, which is why this is hard to read without cringing a bit. Read like that it comes off as "It's very addictive and eventually you get lulled into accepting nonsense again, except I haven't realized that's what's happening".

But the truth is that this comment really relies entirely on what "more and more" means here.

devmor 2026/04/06 02:11
你可以在不理解原因的情况下在某件事上做得更好，但你应该能够相当容易地思考并确定原因。

这是每个关心提高某项技能的人都会定期做的事情——检查他们的进步、背后的原因以及如何加以改进。这是自我驱动学习的基础。

You can get better at something without understanding why, but you should be able to think about it and determine why fairly easily.

This is something everyone who cares about improving in a skill does regularly - examine their improvement, the reasons behind it, and how to add to them. That’s the basis of self-driven learning.

ffsm8 2026/04/06 03:40

在 HN 中查看
devmor 2026/04/06 03:52
我认为你抛弃了模糊的、毫无根据的直觉，并称其为学习。

例如，如果我说“我注意到我穿着蓝色鞋子比穿红色鞋子跑得更好”，我就没有学到任何东西。如果我检查我的鞋子，发现我的蓝色鞋子有缓冲鞋底，而我的红色鞋子是平底鞋，我可以将其与思考我如何跑步结合起来，并了解到缓冲鞋底可以减少我脚和脚踝肌肉的疲劳。

差异很重要的原因是，如果我不执行学习步骤，当我购买另一双蓝色鞋子但它们是平底的时，我就会回到原点。

回到真实的场景，如果你对哪些技巧和措辞有效而抱有毫无根据的直觉，而不理解为什么，你可能会发现这些技巧和措辞在新模型版本上根本不起作用，或者当由于价格、破产等原因被迫更换到不同的产品时。

I think you’re handwaving away vague, ungrounded intuition and calling it learning.

For instance, if I say “I noticed I run better in my blue shoes than my red shoes” I did not learn anything. If I examine my shoes and notice that my blue shoes have a cushioned sole, while my red shoes are flat, I can combine that with thinking about how I run and learn that cushioned soles cause less fatigue to the muscles in my feet and ankles.

The reason the difference matters is because if I don’t do the learning step, when buy another pair of blue shoes but they’re flat soled, I’m back to square one.

Back to the real scenario, if you hold on to your ungrounded intuition re what tricks and phrasing work without understanding why, you may find those don’t work at all on a new model version or when forced to change to a different product due to price, insolvency, etc.

在 HN 中查看
eszed 2026/04/06 14:10
当你发现某个答案令人满意时，你总是可以自由地停留在抽象层次上，但你也可以继续挖掘。为什么平底鞋更好？嗯，这和我的步态有关。好吧，但是为什么我的步态是这样的？肌肉骨骼的东西。为什么我的身体会这样？某种东西——某种遗传的东西。好吧，但这是为什么呢？等等。

如果追求得足够远，任何思路都会得出一些非确定性的东西——或者简单地说，事情就是这样——无论这对于我们这些渴望直接答案的人来说是多么不令人满意。不管你喜欢与否，我们作为人类的基本事实最终取决于直觉。 （随意说，“不，这是物理”，或“不，这是数学”，但我会问你跑步时是否在头脑中进行这些计算！）

You're always free to stop at the level of abstraction at which you find a certain answer to be satisfying, but you can also keep digging. Why are flat shoes better? Well, it's to do with my gait. Ok, but why is my gait like that? Something-something musculoskeletal. Why is my body that way? Something-something genetic. OK, but why is that? And so on.

Pursued far enough, any line of thought will reach something non-deterministic - or, simply, That's The Way It Is - however unsatisfying that is to those of us who crave straightforward answers. Like it or not, our ground truth as human beings ultimately rests on intuition. (Feel free to say, "No, it's physics", or "No, it's maths", but I'll ask you if you're doing those calculations in your head as you run!)

SpicyLemonZest 2026/04/06 10:18
我学到了很多技能，但对我来说，这些技能都没有像你描述的那样有效。直到我在技能树中解锁了斜接接头，我才学会通过随机振动锯木头来切割好的斜接接头。我仔细研究了自己所犯的错误，并以我认为可能纠正这些错误的方式进行调整，其中一些有帮助，有些则无济于事。最终我足够详细地理解了我的行为和基本原则之间的关系，从而始终达到 45 度。
I've learned a number of skills, and for me none of them worked in the way you're describing. I didn't learn to cut good miter joints by randomly vibe-sawing wood until I unlocked miter joints in the skill tree. I carefully studied the errors I made, and adjusted in ways I thought might correct them, some of which helped some of which did not. Then eventually I understood the relationship between my actions and the underlying principles in enough detail to consistently hit 45 degrees.
在 HN 中查看
eszed 2026/04/06 13:50
这个例子不是很简单吗，因为你有一个可直接测量的输出？我的意思是，关节要么是 45°（好吧，90°），要么不是。缩小一点，技能组合就变得更难以定义：我的橱柜是吗？ 好的 - 采用精心挑选的木材和适当的硬件，实现比例匀称、饰面优雅、适合用途的一些交叉点。

请注意，我不认为这些维度的改进过程有本质上的不同，只是不太直接并且不容易（甚至根本）难以表达。

Isn't that example pretty reductive, in that you have a directly-measurable output? I mean, the joint is either 45° (well, 90°) or it's not. Zoom out a bit, and the skill-set becomes much less definable: are my cabinets good - for some intersection of well-proportioned, elegantly-finished, and fit for purpose, with well-chosen wood and appropriate hardware.

Mind you, I don't think the process of improvement in those dimensions is fundamentally different, just much less direct and not easily (or perhaps even at all) articulable.

在 HN 中查看
libraryofbabel 2026/04/06 00:09
同意。这是一篇非常平衡的文章。唯一仍然使这些见解难以应用于专业软件开发的因素是：这是绿地工作，而且是一个单独的项目。但这并不是作者的错。然而，如果能看到更多这样的关于如何在涉及多人的棕地项目中全力使用人工智能工具的文章，那就太棒了。

我要补充的一件事是：我实际上并不认为开始为这样的项目构建一个氛围编码的意大利面条混乱...... 假如 你把它视为一个原型，你可以从中学习然后扔掉。一次性原型非常有用，因为它可以帮助您在下降一个级别并专注于密切指导代理实际构建它之前首先弄清楚您想要构建什么。

作者的错误在于他认为可怕的原型会演变成真实的东西。当然不能。但我怀疑作者重新开始并更加关注架构时的最终结果是 很多 更好是因为他从第一次尝试中更多地了解了他想要构建的需求。

Agree. This is such a good balanced article. The only things that still make the insights difficult to apply to professional software development are: this was greenfield work and it was a solo project. But that’s hardly the author’s fault. It would however be fantastic to see more articles like this about how to go all in on AI tools for brownfield projects involving more than one person.

One thing I will add: I actually don’t think it’s wrong to start out building a vibe coded spaghetti mess for a project like this… provided you see it as a prototype you’re going to learn from and then throw away. A throwaway prototype is immensely useful because it helps you figure out what you want to build in the first place, before you step down a level and focus on closely guiding the agent to actually build it.

The author’s mistake was that he thought the horrible prototype would evolve into the real thing. Of course it could not. But I suspect that the author’s final results when he did start afresh and build with closer attention to architecture were much better because he has learned more about the requirements for what he wanted to build from that first attempt.

在 HN 中查看
argee 2026/04/06 03:01
这甚至不是 只是 这是一项全新的工作，它包括人工智能可以说擅长的工作类型：从现有代码库（SQLite）中提取工作代码作为可重用的库。 （它还包括人工智能真正不擅长的工作类型：明智地设计 API。）
This wasn't even just greenfield work, it included the exact type of work where AI arguably excels: extracting working code from an extant codebase (SQLite) as a reusable library. (It also included the type of work AI is really bad at: designing APIs sensibly.)

csallen 2026/04/06 01:15
我会采取另一面。

像我们许多人一样的专业软件工程师在人工智能编码方面有一个很大的盲点，那就是对代码质量的关注。

关注代码质量是有意义的。我们没有错。毕竟我们已经度过了整个职业生涯 在 代码。糟糕的代码质量会减慢我们的速度，并使最终用户的工作变得缓慢/不安全/不可靠等。

然而，在人工智能编码时代，代码质量变得越来越不重要，忽视这一点就等于把头埋在沙子里。仅仅因为我们不喜欢它并不意味着它不是真的。

有两种力量促成了这一点：(1) 更多的人编写较小的应用程序，(2) 编码模型和代理工具的改进。

我们正日益走向这样一个世界：那些不熟练的程序员正在“构建”自己的应用程序，其用户群只有一个人。在许多情况下，这些应用程序简单而有效，并且没有大型软件套件多年来给用户带来的臃肿感。代码很简单，即使不简单，也没有人需要维护它，所以这并不重要。有些应用程序不可靠，有些应用程序会被黑客攻击，有些应用程序速度缓慢且效率低下，但这并不重要。这种趋势将继续增长。

与此同时，技术在不断进步，人工智能越来越擅长设计和构建软件。我们正处于人工智能的最初几个月，实际上在这方面有一定的能力。它不太可能会陷入停滞并停止改进。即使最终实现了，如果到了这一点，工具仍然需要很多年的改进，因为人类有效利用技术的能力总是远远落后于技术本身的发明。

所以我和你们一样对所有的炒作和夸张的说法感到恼火。但人工智能辅助编码的“真相”每年、每个季度、每个月都在变化。它只朝一个方向发展。而且它不会停止。

I'll take the other side of this.

Professional software engineers like many of us have a big blind spot when it comes to AI coding, and that's a fixation on code quality.

It makes sense to focus on code quality. We're not wrong. After all, we've spent our entire careers in the code. Bad code quality slows us down and makes things slow/insecure/unreliable/etc for end users.

However, code quality is becoming less and less relevant in the age of AI coding, and to ignore that is to have our heads stuck in the sand. Just because we don't like it doesn't mean it's not true.

There are two forces contributing to this: (1) more people coding smaller apps, and (2) improvements in coding models and agentic tools.

We are increasingly moving toward a world where people who aren't sophisticated programmers are "building" their own apps with a user base of just one person. In many cases, these apps are simple and effective and come without the bloat that larger software suites have subjected users to for years. The code is simple, and even when it's not, nobody will ever have to maintain it, so it doesn't matter. Some apps will be unreliable, some will get hacked, some will be slow and inefficient, and it won't matter. This trend will continue to grow.

At the same time, technology is improving, and the AI is increasingly good at designing and architecting software. We are in the very earliest months of AI actually being somewhat competent at this. It's unlikely that it will plateau and stop improving. And even when it finally does, if such a point comes, there will still be many years of improvements in tooling, as humanity's ability to make effective use of a technology always lags far behind the invention of the technology itself.

So I'm right there with you in being annoyed by all the hype and exaggerated claims. But the "truth" about AI-assisted coding is changing every year, every quarter, every month. It's only trending in one direction. And it isn't going to stop.

在 HN 中查看
mjr00 2026/04/06 02:07
> 然而，在人工智能编码时代，代码质量变得越来越不重要，忽视这一点就是把我们的头埋在沙子里。仅仅因为我们不喜欢它并不意味着它不是真的。

强烈不同意这个论点，事实上我会完全相反：由于人工智能，代码质量比以往任何时候都更加重要。

LLM 辅助编码在具有与高代码质量密切相关的属性的代码库中最为成功：可预测的模式、命名良好的变量、类型系统的使用、无全局可变状态、总体可变性非常低等。

我现在正在 Python 代码库的一个相当糟糕的遗留区域上使用人工智能（就像，实际上现在，当我输入这个时，克劳德正在运行），并且它的挣扎与人类挣扎的原因相同。这个 DataFrame 中有哪些列？谁知道呢，因为数据帧会根据函数调用而发生变化！哦，是的，有人认为他们可以“聪明”，通过字符串组装函数名称并动态调用它们以节省几行代码，太棒了！与任何人一样，法学硕士将很难破译这个灾难性的作品。

与此同时，对于具有严格类型和合理架构的新代码区域，无论我提出什么要求，克劳德通常都会一次性完成。

编辑：我看到大多数回复都说基本上相同的事情，这是一个指标。

> However, code quality is becoming less and less relevant in the age of AI coding, and to ignore that is to have our heads stuck in the sand. Just because we don't like it doesn't mean it's not true.

Strongly disagree with this thesis, and in fact I'd go completely the opposite: code quality is more important than ever thanks to AI.

LLM-assisted coding is most successful in codebases with attributes strongly associated with high code quality: predictable patterns, well-named variables, use of a type system, no global mutable state, very low mutability in general, etc.

I'm using AI on a pretty shitty legacy area of a Python codebase right now (like, literally right now, Claude is running while I type this) and it's struggling for the same reason a human would struggle. What are the columns in this DataFrame? Who knows, because the dataframe is getting mutated depending on the function calls! Oh yeah and someone thought they could be "clever" and assemble function names via strings and dynamically call them to save a few lines of code, awesome! An LLM is going to struggle deciphering this disasterpiece, same as anyone.

Meanwhile for newer areas of the code with strict typing and a sensible architecture, Claude will usually just one-shot whatever I ask.

edit: I see most replies are saying basically the same thing here, which is an indicator.

在 HN 中查看
59nadir 2026/04/06 15:08
我完全同意你的说法，即结构使法学硕士和人类的事情变得更容易，但我会温和地反对这种突变。正如突变对人类来说很好一样，对于法学硕士来说似乎也很好，因为结构化突变（我们知道我们可以改变什么，我们可以改变它以及改变什么）效果很好。

您的数据帧示例是动态语言及其敏感性的典型的完全非结构化突变。

我从经验中知道，现代模型（即使是便宜的模型）都没有处理全局或近全局状态并改变它的问题，甚至导航互斥体/互斥体、条件等。

I agree entirely with your statement that structure makes things easier for both LLMs and humans, but I'd gently push back on the mutation. Exactly as mutation is fine for humans it also seems to be fine for LLMs in that structured mutation (we know what we can change, where we can change it and to what) works just fine.

Your example with the dataframes is completely unstructured mutation typical of a dynamic language and its sensibilities.

I know from experience that none of the modern models (even cheap ones) have issues dealing with global or near-global state and mutating it, even navigating mutexes/mutices, conds, and so on.

在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 07:50
> LLM 辅助编码在具有与高代码质量密切相关的属性的代码库中最为成功：可预测的模式、命名良好的变量、类型系统的使用、无全局可变状态、一般来说非常低的可变性等。

这都是事实，但您忽略的是，需要此功能的代码库的比例相对于代码库总数正在缩小。非常小的、定制的、简单的、人工智能编码的应用程序数量惊人地激增，但它们仍然非常有用。大多数都是由那些一生中从未写过一行代码的人创建的，他们不会进行任何维护，也不会对代码的外观给出两个废话，就像普通的 YouTuber 关心他们的镜头光圈或普通的论坛评论者关心他们的散文风格一样。

我们看不到这些应用程序是因为我们是从事其他工作的专业软件工程师。但我们正在迅速接近这样一个世界：越来越多的软件是由 非-专业人士。

> LLM-assisted coding is most successful in codebases with attributes strongly associated with high code quality: predictable patterns, well-named variables, use of a type system, no global mutable state, very low mutability in general, etc.

That's all very true, but what you're missing is that the proportion of codebases that need this is shrinking relative to the total number of codebases. There's an incredible proliferation of very small, bespoke, simple, AI-coded apps, that are nonetheless quite useful. Most are being created by people who have never written a line of code in their life, who will do no maintenance, and who will not give two craps how the code looks, any more than the average YouTuber cares about the aperture of their lens or the average forum commenter care about the style of their prose.

We don't see these apps because we're professional software engineers working on the other stuff. But we're rapidly approaching a world where more and more software is created by non-professionals.

在 HN 中查看
mjr00 2026/04/06 08:18
> 这一切都非常正确，但您缺少的是，需要此功能的代码库的比例相对于代码库总数正在缩小。非常小的、定制的、简单的、人工智能编码的应用程序数量惊人地激增，但它们仍然非常有用。大多数都是由那些一生中从未写过一行代码的人创建的，他们不会进行任何维护，也不会对代码的外观给出两个废话，就像普通的 YouTuber 关心他们的镜头光圈或普通的论坛评论者关心他们的散文风格一样。

我同意将会有更多小型的一次性实用程序，但您似乎认为这会减少传统长期代码库的数量或重要性，这是没有意义的。 Jane Q. Notadeveloper 可以通过 Vibe 编写一个用于跟踪家务的应用程序，这一点很棒，但这并没有改变这样一个事实：她需要使用她的操作系统（一个庞大的代码库）来打开 Google Chrome（一个庞大的代码库），然后访问她的银行网站（一个庞大的代码库），将钱转给她的房东以出租（这个过程涉及许多彼此交互的大型软件系统，希望没有一个是 Vivi 编码的）。

一个恰当的比喻是，一般 YouTuber 不关心镜头光圈：YouTube 视频的平均观看次数为 35 次[0]。这些人可能不关心他们的相机或音频设置，这是事实。问题是，这与真正的专业 YouTube 主播 MrBeast 等人有什么关系？ 做 关心他们的 AV 设置吗？

[0] https://www.intotheminds.com/blog/en/research-youtube-stats/

> That's all very true, but what you're missing is that the proportion of codebases that need this is shrinking relative to the total number of codebases. There's an incredible proliferation of very small, bespoke, simple, AI-coded apps, that are nonetheless quite useful. Most are being created by people who have never written a line of code in their life, who will do no maintenance, and who will not give two craps how the code looks, any more than the average YouTuber cares about the aperture of their lens or the average forum commenter care about the style of their prose.

I agree that there will be more small, single-use utilities, but you seem to believe that this will decrease the number or importance of traditional long-lived codebases, which doesn't make sense. The fact that Jane Q. Notadeveloper can vibe code an app for tracking household chores is great, but it does not change the fact that she needs to use her operating system (a massive codebase) to open Google Chrome (a massive codebase) and go to her bank's website (a massive codebase) to transfer money to her landlord for rent (a process which involves many massive software systems interacting with each other, hopefully none of which are vibe coded).

The average YouTuber not caring about the aperture of their lens is an apt comparison: the median YouTube video has 35 views[0]. These people likely do not care about their camera or audio setup, it's true. The question is, how is that relevant to the actual professional YouTubers, MrBeast et al, who actually do care about their AV setup?

[0] https://www.intotheminds.com/blog/en/research-youtube-stats/

在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 09:22
这是我进入更多投机领域的地方，但我认为人们低估了人工智能助手应用程序对传统软件行业的蚕食程度。就像智能手机吞噬了许多个人工具、计算器、秒表、iPod 等一样。

人类需要很长时间才能适应新技术。首先，技术需要多年改进。然后它需要被采用并接近普遍存在。然后社会中行动缓慢的部分需要围绕它聚集和重新安排。例如，网络在 90 年代中期已经为 Airbnb 等应用程序做好了准备，但采用+文化+基础设施还没有准备好。

在 5 年，也许 10 年，当然 15 年里，我认为不会有那么多人愿意学习、浏览和点击无数复杂的网站、应用程序和流程，因为他们可以轻松地告诉他们的助手来完成大部分工作。谷歌已经正确地认识到这是一种生存威胁，许多 SaaS 公司也是如此。

人工智能助手已经足够好，可以动态创建临时应用程序来回答某些问题。我们正处于人们建立供法学硕士使用的企业和基础设施的非常非常早期的时期。

This is where I get into much more speculative land, but I think people are underestimating the degree to which AI assistant apps are going to eat much of the traditional software industry. The same way smart phones ate so many individual tools, calculators, stop watches, iPods, etc.

It takes a long time for humanity to adjust to a new technology. First, the technology needs to improve for years. Then it needs to be adopted and reach near ubiquity. And then the slower-moving parts of society need to converge and rearrange around it. For example, the web was quite ready for apps like Airbnb in the mid 90s, but the adoption+culture+infra was not.

In 5, maybe 10, certainly 15 years, I don't think as many people are going to want to learn, browse, and click through a gazillion complex websites and apps and flows when they can easily just tell their assistant to do most of it. Google already correctly realizes this as an existential threat, as do many SaaS companies.

AI assistants are already good enough to create ephemeral applications on the fly in response to certain questions. And we're in the very, very early days of people building businesses and infra meant to be consumed by LLMs.

mjr00 2026/04/06 10:00
> 在 5 年，也许 10 年，当然 15 年里，我认为不会有那么多人愿意学习、浏览和点击无数复杂的网站、应用程序和流程，因为他们可以轻松地告诉他们的助手来完成大部分工作。

您认为他们的助手将如何与外部系统交互？如果我告诉我的人工智能助手“支付我的租金”或“预订我的航班”，你认为它会在银行和航空公司的服务器上短暂地编码一些东西来实现这一点吗？

您只想到冰山一角，这是面向客户的软件的最后一英里。 90% 以上的软件开发是冰山的其余部分，在表面之下是看不见的。

我同意会有 更多的 但这并不排除更多现有大型后端系统的存在。

> In 5, maybe 10, certainly 15 years, I don't think as many people are going to want to learn, browse, and click through a gazillion complex websites and apps and flows when they can easily just tell their assistant to do most of it.

And how do you think their assistant will interact with external systems? If I tell my AI assistant "pay my rent" or "book my flight" do you think it's going to ephemerally vibe code something on the banks' and airlines' servers to make this happen?

You're only thinking of the tip of the iceberg which is the last mile of client-facing software. 90%+ of software development is the rest of the iceberg, unseen beneath the surface.

I agree there will be more of this but again, that does not preclude the existence of more of the big backend systems existing.

I don't think we disagree. We still have big mainframe systems from the 70s and beyond that a powering parts of society. I don't think all current software systems are just going to die or disappear, especially not the big ones. But I do think significant double digit percentages of software engineers are working on other types of software that are at risk of becoming first- or second- or third-order casualties in a world where ephemeral AI assistant-generated software and vibe coded bespoke software becomes increasingly popular.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/06 02:13
然而，在人工智能编码时代，代码质量变得越来越不重要

它实际上变成了 越来越多 相关的。人工智能需要不断地重新阅读自己的代码，并将其融入到有限的上下文中，以便将其作为编写新东西的参考。 这意味着每一个代码味道，以及每一个不必要的代码膨胀实例，实际上都会成为进一步发展的严重危险。 可以说，你实际上应该非常痴迷于重构和清理人工智能所提出的东西，甚至比你纯粹为人类编码更是如此。

However, code quality is becoming less and less relevant in the age of AI coding

It actually becomes more and more relevant. AI constantly needs to reread its own code and fit it into its limited context, in order to take it as a reference for writing out new stuff. This means that every single code smell, and every instance of needless code bloat, actually becomes a grievous hazard to further progress. Arguably, you should in fact be quite obsessed about refactoring and cleaning up what the AI has come up with, even more so than if you were coding purely for humans.

bananamogul 2026/04/06 11:37
即使是非前沿模型现在也提供 100 万个令牌的上下文窗口。 那是 100K-300K LOC。 我不会称其为有限的背景。
Even non-frontier models now offer a context window of 1 million tokens. That's 100K-300K LOCs. I would not call that a limited context.

vlovich123 2026/04/06 01:23
> 然而，在人工智能编码时代，代码质量变得越来越不重要，忽视这一点就是把我们的头埋在沙子里。仅仅因为我们不喜欢它并不意味着它不是真的。

强烈不同意。我刚刚看到一个团队花了数周的时间试图让一段代码与人工智能一起工作，因为编码的氛围是意大利面条垃圾，甚至人工智能也无法告诉需要做什么，并且基本上在玩无效的whackamole - 它会通过重新引入旧错误或引入新错误来修复你提出的错误，因为没有人理解发生了什么。人类甚至无法像平常一样介入，因为没有人明白发生了什么。

> However, code quality is becoming less and less relevant in the age of AI coding, and to ignore that is to have our heads stuck in the sand. Just because we don't like it doesn't mean it's not true.

Strong disagree. I just watched a team spend weeks trying to make a piece of code work with AI because the vibe coded was spaghetti garbage that even the AI couldn’t tell what needed to be done and was basically playing ineffective whackamole - it would fix the bug you ask it by reintroducing an old bug or introducing a new bug because no one understood what was happening. And humans couldn’t even step in like normal because no one understood what’s going on.

vlovich123 2026/04/06 03:13
我认为代码质量仍然非常重要，并且只会继续重要。

人工智能将更擅长编写良好的可维护和可解释的代码，因为这是真正轻松解决问题所需要的。但“代码质量并不重要，因为人工智能”的说法无论从经验上还是从预测上来说都绝对不正确。未来人工智能会做得更好吗？当然。但因为他们的代码质量提高了，并不是因为它不那么重要了。

Im arguing that code quality very much still matters and will only continue to matter.

AI will get better at making good maintainable and explainable code because that’s what it takes to actually solve problems tractably. But saying “code quality doesn’t matter because AI” is definitely not true both experientially and as a prediction. Will AI do a better job in the future? Sure. But because their code quality improves not because it’s less important.

csallen 2026/04/06 06:24
越来越多的软件将由非专家构建，这些软件具有较小的用户群和更简单的用例，并且不需要太多维护（如果有的话）。 “人工智能代码质量差”对这些人来说远不如 FAANG 公司的开发人员编写的软件那么重要，因为实际上没有人会看代码。

我们正走向一个拥有大量软件的世界 短暂的，人工智能凭空创建的应用程序，仅供一次性使用，然后就消失了。

More and more software will be built by non-experts, software that has smaller user bases and simpler use cases and doesn't need to be maintained as much if at all. "Poor AI code quality" matters much less for these than for say, software written by developers at FAANG companies, since literally nobody will ever even look at the code.

Where we're headed is toward a world where a ton of software is ephemeral, apps literally created by AI out of thin air for a single use, and then gone.

fn-mote 2026/04/06 09:09
短暂的就像老房子里的电线是短暂的一样。

也就是说，根本不是。

最初的接线由专业人士完成，后来由“氛围电工”房主进行更改。

每条赛道都可能是一项定制工作，但它们都会累积成 SWE 所说的“技术债务”。

不喜欢烤面包机和微波炉位于同一电路上，即使它们位于厨房的不同部分？如果您甚至可以沿着接线回到电路盒，看看它是如何完成的，那您就很幸运了。配电箱太乱了，你哪里会运行新电路？

这就是我们所期待的未来。

Ephemeral in the same way the electrical wiring in an old house is ephemeral.

Which is to say, not at all.

Original wiring done by a professional, later changes by “vibe electrician” homeowners.

Every circuit might be a custom job, but they all accumulate into something a SWE calls “technical debt”.

Don’t like how the toaster and the microwave are on the same circuit even though they are in different parts of the kitchen? You’re lucky if you can even follow the wiring back to the circuit box to see how it was done. The electrical box is so much of a mess where would you even run a new circuit?

That’s the future we’re looking at.

在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 09:27
不会是短暂的：我会要求人工智能检查我的电子邮件，它会在我的人工智能助手中动态创建一个定制的表格 UI，并用相关的电子邮件数据填充它。我会使用它，然后它就会消失。软件的创建和销毁都在一瞬间。

并非所有软件都是其他软件所依赖的永久构建块。

当新技术的到来使早期的做事方式变得过时时，历史上的一致模式是现有的专家和专业人士大大低估了即将到来的变化，很大程度上是因为（a）他们不喜欢这些变化，（b）他们太习惯了各种限制和优先事项，这些限制和优先事项曾经很重要，但现在不再重要。换句话说，他们是通过旧世界的视角来判断新技术，而不是通过新技术创造的新世界的视角。

No ephemeral as in: I'll ask the AI to check my email, and it'll create a bespoke table UI on the fly right inside my AI assistant, and populate it with relevant email data. And I'll use it, and then it will disappear. Software created and destroyed in a moment.

Not all software is meant to be some permanent building block upon which other software sits.

When new technology arrives that makes earlier ways of doing things obsolete, the consistent pattern throughout history has been that existing experts and professionals significantly underestimate the changes to come, in large part because (a) they don't like those changes, and (b) they're too used to various constraints and priorities that used to be important but no longer are. In other words, they're judging the new tech the lens of an older world, rather than through the lens of a newer world created by the new tech.

There's almost no point in arguing about this anymore. Neither you nor the other person are going to be convinced. We just have to wait and see if a new crop of 100x productivity AI believer companies come along and unseat all the incumbents.

layer8 2026/04/06 01:45

It seems that your opinion is based on expectations for the future then, which is notoriously difficult to predict.
在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 01:48
不难预测，明显有用的新技术会随着时间的推移而改进。

枪、轮子、汽车、轮船、电池、电视、互联网、智能手机、飞机、制冷、电力照明、半导体、GPS、太阳能电池板、抗生素、印刷机、蒸汽机、无线电等。模式明显，力量清晰且经过充分研究。

如果（1）当前能力与理论极限之间存在巨大差距，（2）对那些改进事物的人有巨大的激励，（3）没有替代技术可以取代或超越它，（4）广泛的社会接受和采用，以及（5）技术不会丢失或遗忘，那么技术改进基本上是一种保证。

这些对于人工智能编码来说显然都是正确的。

It's not that hard to predict that obviously useful new technology is going to improve over time.

Guns, wheels, cars, ships, batteries, televisions, the internet, smartphones, airplanes, refrigeration, electric lighting, semiconductors, GPS, solar panels, antibiotics, printing presses, steam engines, radio, etc. The pattern is obvious, the forces are clear and well-studied.

If there is (1) a big gap between current capabilities and theoretical limits, (2) huge incentives for those who to improve things, (3) no alternative tech that will replace or outcompete it, (4) broad social acceptance and adoption, and (5) no chance of the tech being lost or forgotten, then technological improvement is basically a guarantee.

These are all obviously true of AI coding.

在 HN 中查看
ThrowawayR2 2026/04/06 05:27
该列表精选了所有技术改进的成功案例，而忽略了许多其他技术没有改进且技术没有进一步改进的案例。 这是不诚实的。

这甚至不是一个很好的挑选工作：在协和式飞机之后，我们再也没有得到主流的超音速客机，因为航空航天技术还没有发展到足以使其在经济上可行的程度，而且众所周知，尖端工艺的半导体的进度下降和成本大幅增加。

That list cherry picks all the successful cases where the technology improved while ignoring the many, many others where it didn't and the technology improved no further. That's dishonest.

It isn't even a good job of cherry picking: we never got mainstream supersonic passenger aircraft after the Concorde because aerospace technology hasn't advanced far enough to make it economically viable and the decrease in progress and massively increasing costs in semiconductors for cutting edge processes is very well known.

在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 05:59
您没有考虑我提供的限制列表。

超音速飞行并没有得到广泛的社会接受，因为它会产生公众不想处理的令人难以置信的巨大音爆。尽管如此，这仍然是一个糟糕的反例，因为公司在这一领域不断创新，例如轰隆超音速。

充其量你可以说，“它花费的时间比预期的要长”，但我的观点绝不是它会按照任何特定的时间表发生。枪支从中国原始的火枪发展到1400年代带有锁定机构的武器，用了400年的时间。那些漫长的时间框架只会更加有力地证明我的观点。只要有胃口、有接受、有激励、有成长空间，而且时间不是障碍，进步就会发生。这是人类历史上最确定的事情之一，其背后的力量已经得到了很好的研究。

同样可以肯定的是：被这些新技术淘汰的人和工作往往仍然否认，直到被遗忘。

You're not factoring in the list of constraints I provided.

There's no broad social acceptance of supersonic flight because it creates incredibly loud sonic booms that the public doesn't want to deal with. And despite that, it's still a bad counterexample, as companies continue to innovate in this area e.g. Boom Supersonic.

At best you can say, "It's taking longer than expected," but my point was never that it will happen on any specific schedule. It took 400 years for guns to advance from the primitive fire lances in China to weapons with lock mechanisms in the 1400s. Those long time frames only prove my point even more strongly. Progress WILL happen, when there is appetite and acceptance and incentive and room to grow, and time is no obstacle. It's one of the more certain things in human history, and the forces behind it have been well studies.

Just as certain: the people and jobs who are obsoleted by these new technologies often remain in denial until they are forgotten.

NoMoreNicksLeft 2026/04/06 11:55
报纸行业是一个完美的比喻，因为它实际上已经死了。批发死了。到处都有最大的、世界上最知名的报纸仍然活着，依靠生命支持……《纽约时报》、《华尔街日报》等。但它们都死了。他们的死亡导致了整个工业部门的彻底毁灭，并给邻近的工业带来了坏疽，而他们很快就会屈服。 1998 年的重点并不在于这种转变需要仔细关注和明智的策略，而是无论任何人采取什么措施来阻止它，死亡都将到来。

报纸的消亡也是一个奇观。似乎没有人明白这有多糟糕……最年轻的一代似乎甚至无法意识到缺少了什么。我们实际上已经将新闻业业余化了，这样，只有骗子和无能​​的黑客才愿意尝试，而且只是在推特或其他社交媒体上的小片段中（他们很快就会发现为外国政府或传销江湖骗术进行宣传可能更有利可图）。当软件行业彻底消亡时，它也将完全业余化，最年轻的一代甚至不会意识到人们曾经以软件为生，而少数从事软件行业的业余爱好者将开始理解仅仅制作伪装不良的恶意软件会更有利可图。

The newspaper industry is the perfect analogy, because it is effectively dead. Wholesale dead. Here and there, the biggest, most world-renowned papers are still alive, on life-support... NYT, WSJ, etc. But they're all dead. Their death has caused the absolute destruction of an entire industry sector and has given gangrene to adjacent industries that they will soon succumb to. The point about 1998 wasn't that there was this transition that demanded careful attention and wise strategy, but that death was coming for it no matter what anyone did to stop it.

The death of newspapers is quite the spectacle too. No one seems to understand how bad it is... the youngest generation can't even seem to recognize that anything is missing. We've effectively amateurized journalism so that only grifters and talentless hacks want to attempt it, and only in tiny little soundbites on Twitter or other social media (and they're quickly finding out how it might be more lucrative to do propaganda for foreign governments or MLM charlatanism). When the death of the software industry is complete, it too will have been completely amateurized, the youngest generation will not even appreciate that people used to make it for a living, and the few amateurs doing it will start to comprehend how much more lucrative it will be to just make poorly disguised malware.

在 HN 中查看
Philip-J-Fry 2026/04/06 01:40
我根本不买这个。代码质量永远很重要。对于法学硕士来说，语境是王道，当你用数千行意大利面条填满语境时，法学硕士将会（而且确实）表现更差。垃圾进来，垃圾出去，这仍然是我的经验的真理。

意大利面条代码仍然是意大利面条代码。一些本来应该很小的改变最终会触及代码库的多个部分。这不仅会增加成本，而且在您下次需要更改此功能时还会变得更加复杂。

我不明白为什么这会成为任何人想要的现实。如果更简单的代码可以更快、更便宜地找到答案，为什么还要让代理兜圈子、烧钱并最终找到答案呢？

也许有一天它会改变。也许会有一种新的人工智能技术彻底改变我们的工作方式。但如果法学硕士的架构保持不变，我不明白为什么你不想有效地利用上下文窗口。

I don't buy this at all. Code quality will always matter. Context is king with LLMs, and when you fill that context up with thousands of lines of spaghetti, the LLM will (and does) perform worse. Garbage in, garbage out, that's still the truth from my experience.

Spaghetti code is still spaghetti code. Something that should be a small change ends up touching multiple parts of the codebase. Not only does this increase costs, it just compounds the next time you need to change this feature.

I don't see why this would be a reality that anyone wants. Why would you want an agent going in circles, burning money and eventually finding the answer, if simpler code could get it there faster and cheaper?

Maybe one day it'll change. Maybe there will be a new AI technology which shakes up the whole way we do it. But if the architecture of LLMs stays as it is, I don't see why you wouldn't want to make efficient use of the context window.

在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 01:46
我并没有说你“想要”意大利面条代码或者意大利面条代码很好。

我说过（a）应用程序变得越来越简单，范围越来越小，因此它们的代码质量不再那么重要，（b）人工智能在编写良好的代码方面变得越来越好。

I didn't say that you "want" spaghetti code or that spaghetti code is good.

I said that (a) apps are getting simpler and smaller in scope and so their code quality matters less, and (b) AI is getting better at writing good code.

pessimizer 2026/04/06 06:41
这些主张与您所回复的评论的主张有什么相关性？

你是否想暗示拥有更多的东西意味着它们每一个都会变小？与 500 年前相比，现在的人口数量更多了——是变小了，还是变大了？

还有印刷机 做过 导致更长的作品。有许多连续的丛书已经运行了几十年，有几十卷、几百万字。这是印刷机的直接结果。就像有些电视节目的情节连续播放数千小时一样。这是视频录制和制作技术的结果；舞台剧做不到这一点。

您似乎试图在没有支持的情况下将“范围较小”纳入您的声明中，尽管我坚持认为个人编写的应用程序“范围较小”是可用工具的明显结果。我不可能知道一切，所以我必须将语言和技术限制在我所知道的范围内，而且我写得不够快，无法让事情变得庞大。我选择解决的问题就是基于这些限制。

这些正是法学硕士应该改变的事情。

What relevance do either of those claims have to the claim of the comment you are responding to?

Are you trying to imply that having more things means that each of them will be smaller? There are more people than there were 500 years ago - are they smaller, or larger?

Also, the printing press did lead to much longer works. There are many continuous book series that have run for decades, with dozens of volumes and millions of words. This is a direct result of the printing press. Just as there are television shows that have run with continuous plots for thousands of hours. This is a consequence of video recording and production technologies; you couldn't do that with stage plays.

You seem to be trying to slip "smaller in scope" into your statement without backing, even though I'd insist that applications individuals wrote being "smaller in scope" was a obvious consequence of the tooling available. I can't know everything, so I have to keep the languages and techniques limited to the ones that I do know, and I can't write fast enough to make things huge. The problems I choose to tackle are based on those restrictions.

Those are the exact things that LLMs are meant to change.

在 HN 中查看
csallen 2026/04/06 07:13
今天撰写和出版的平均文章比过去的平均文章短得多。看看推特。一般社交媒体。互联网论坛。博客文章。电子邮件。聊天。等等。此内容的数量使其他内容相形见绌。

大多数民主化的事情也是如此。看视频。 TikTok、YouTube、YouTube 短片。

看看人们正在使用人工智能为自己构建的所有应用程序。他们通常不会构建 Microsoft Word。

当然会有 一些 应用程序比以往任何时候都更大、更雄心勃勃。我自己目前正在构建一个应用程序，它比我在没有人工智能的情况下尝试构建的应用程序更大、更雄心勃勃。我很清楚这个用例。

但正如许多人指出的那样，人工智能在这些方面比在小型应用程序方面表现更差。在我看来，假装这些是唯一重要的应用程序是导致开发人员高估代码质量重要性的原因。目前大多数专业工程师都看不到正在发生的事情，即非开发人员快速构建的定制个人应用程序的时间激增，这将削弱人们购买和使用拥有数十万用户的大型、臃肿的专业软件的理由。

The average piece written and published today today is much shorter than the average piece from the past. Look at Twitter. Social media in general. Internet forums. Blog posts. Emails. Chats. Etc. The amount of this content DWARFS other content.

The same is true of most things that get democratized. Look at video. TikTok, YouTube, YouTube shorts.

Look at all the apps people are building are building for themselves with AI. They are typically not building Microsoft Word.

Of course there will be some apps that are bigger and more ambitious than ever. I myself am currently building an app that's bigger an more ambitious than I would have tried to build without AI. I'm well aware of this use case.

But as many have pointed out, AI is worse at these than at smaller apps. And pretending that these are the only apps that matter is what's leading developers imo to over-value the importance of code quality. What's happening right now that's invisible to most professional engineers is an explosion in the number of time, bespoke personal applications being quickly built by non-developers are that are going to chip away at people's reasons to buy and use large, bloated, professional software with hundreds of thousands of users.

csallen 2026/04/06 05:36
这是杰文斯悖论的错误应用。我们不会得到更大的应用程序，我们会得到 更多的 应用程序。

想想当我们从抄写员到印刷机，再从印刷机到网络时，写作发生了什么。书籍和论文并没有变得更大。我们只是让更多的人写作。

That's the incorrect application of the Jevons Paradox. We won't get bigger apps, we'll get more apps.

Think about what happened to writing when we went from scribes to the printing press, and from the printing press to the web. Books and essays didn't get bigger. We just got more people writing.

在 HN 中查看
vehementi 2026/04/06 09:42
你写的任何内容似乎都不能支持你一开始所说的内容。为什么代码质量的重要性下降了？
Nothing you wrote seems to support what you said at the start there. Why is the importance of code quality decreasing?
